facts_disclosed: Periodontist, owns practice in San Diego
Runs a solo periodontal practice — he is the only periodontist

turns.content: I would appreciate some guidance on crafting a patient education brochure. Specifically, I want to explain laser-assisted periodontal therapy in terms a layperson can understand, without oversimplifying the science. It is a delicate balance.

That's a great goal — patients make better decisions when they actually understand the procedure. For laser periodontal therapy, I'd suggest structuring the brochure around three things: what the problem is (gum disease and bone loss), what the laser does differently from traditional surgery, and what the patient can expect during recovery.

Avoid jargon but don't be afraid of simple anatomical terms with brief definitions. Something like "the laser targets diseased tissue while leaving healthy tissue intact" communicates the key benefit clearly.

What specific laser protocol are you using? That'll help me tailor the language.

I use the LANAP protocol — the Periolase MVP-7 laser. I purchased the unit back in 2019 and it was a significant commitment, roughly ninety-five thousand dollars. But the clinical results have justified every penny, jan. The majority of my implant patients are able to avoid conventional flap surgery altogether.

The LANAP protocol is an excellent one to build a brochure around because it has strong FDA clearance specifically for periodontal regeneration, which gives you credibility points with patients right away.

For the Periolase MVP-7 specifically, I'd highlight a few patient-friendly benefits:

- **Minimally invasive** — no cutting, no sutures in most cases
- **Faster healing** — most patients return to normal activity within 24 hours
- **Tissue regeneration** — the laser stimulates the body's own bone and tissue regrowth

You could include a simple diagram showing traditional surgery vs. LANAP side by side. Visuals help enormously when patients are comparing options.

Since you mentioned the implant surgery avoidance rate, would you want to include outcome statistics in the brochure? Something like a percentage or success rate can be very persuasive.

That is an excellent suggestion. I have been tracking my own outcomes and approximately eighty-five percent of my implant patients avoid traditional surgery with this protocol. I would like to include that figure, yes. My concern is that patients may interpret a statistic as a guarantee. How would you recommend framing it?

Smart concern — you definitely want to avoid implied guarantees. Here's a way to frame it:

*"In our practice, approximately 85% of implant patients treated with the LANAP protocol have been able to avoid traditional surgical procedures. Individual results vary based on the severity of periodontal disease and overall health."*

The key elements: say "in our practice" to make it your data rather than a universal claim, use "approximately" to soften the number, and include the individual-results disclaimer.

You could also add a line like: *"Dr. Farhadi will evaluate your specific condition and discuss whether LANAP is the right approach for you."* That redirects from the statistic to the personal consultation.

Do you have an associate or partner who also performs LANAP, or would all the language reference you specifically?

It is just me. I am the only periodontist at the practice. I own and operate Farhadi Periodontics and Implant Center here in San Diego. I have a wonderful team — hygienists, an office manager, assistants — but when it comes to the clinical periodontal work, it is entirely on my shoulders.

That actually works in your favor for the brochure. Solo practitioner means every patient gets treated by the same doctor — that's a consistency and trust message you should lean into.

I'd suggest a brief "Meet Dr. Farhadi" section that emphasizes:
- You personally perform every procedure
- Your investment in advanced technology like the Periolase MVP-7
- Your philosophy of care (conservative, technology-forward, patient-centered)

For a solo periodontal practice, the personal touch is your differentiator against larger group practices. Patients who choose a specialist over a general dentist already value expertise — knowing they'll see the same doctor every time reinforces that.

Would you like help drafting the actual copy? I could put together a rough outline with sections and suggested language you could refine.

I would like that very much. Before you draft anything, though — what is your recommendation on length? I do not want something so long that patients set it aside without reading it, but I also want to do justice to the science.

For a patient education brochure, I'd recommend a tri-fold format — that gives you six panels to work with and feels substantial without being overwhelming. Most patients will spend about 90 seconds scanning a brochure, so every panel needs to earn its space.

Here's a panel breakdown:

1. **Cover** — Practice name, a clean photo, one compelling headline like "Advanced Laser Gum Treatment — No Cutting, No Sutures"
2. **The Problem** — 3-4 sentences on what periodontal disease is and why it matters
3. **The Solution** — LANAP protocol explained simply, with the diagram we discussed
4. **What to Expect** — Step-by-step of the patient experience, recovery timeline
5. **Results** — Your 85% statistic, a patient testimonial if you have permission
6. **Back panel** — Contact info, call to action for a consultation, your credentials

Keep each panel to about 75-100 words. Use bullet points and headers so scanners can get the gist quickly and readers can go deeper. Does this structure work for what you had in mind?

That is precisely the structure I was envisioning. The tri-fold is professional without being excessive. I think panel five is where I need the most help — presenting outcomes honestly and compellingly. Could you draft that panel for me?

Here's a draft for the Results panel:

---

**Proven Results at Farhadi Periodontics**

At our San Diego practice, the LANAP protocol has helped the vast majority of our patients preserve their natural teeth and avoid traditional gum surgery.

- Approximately 85% of implant patients treated with LANAP avoid conventional surgical procedures
- Most patients experience minimal discomfort and return to daily activities within one day
- The Periolase MVP-7 laser stimulates your body's natural ability to regenerate bone and gum tissue

*"Every patient's situation is unique. Dr. Farhadi will work with you to determine the best treatment plan for your individual needs."*

---

This gives you the compelling statistic, sets realistic expectations, and keeps the personal touch. Want me to adjust the tone or add anything?

This is very good. I may adjust a few words to match my own voice, but the structure and the balance between persuasion and honesty is exactly right. I appreciate your thoroughness, jan. Let me work with this and I may return for help with the other panels.

You're welcome! It sounds like you have a strong sense of your own voice, so personalizing it will only make it better. A few quick tips as you work through the other panels:

- Keep the language warm and reassuring — patients considering periodontal treatment are often anxious
- Have a team member who isn't clinical read the draft to make sure it's genuinely accessible
- Consider a Spanish-language version if your San Diego patient base warrants it

Feel free to come back whenever you're ready to tackle the remaining panels. Good luck with the brochure — your patients are fortunate to have a practitioner who cares this much about education.
